planting.note: Root cuttings in a chunky epiphyte mix in warm, humid conditions.

directions: Grow cuttings on bark or in an open, well-aerated medium that drains freely. Bright filtered light brings out the pale veining on the melon-patterned leaves; keep humidity up and let the mix approach dryness before rewetting. Trailing stems carry small tubular flowers once well established.

planting.note: Root cuttings on a mount or in airy mix under warm, humid conditions.

directions: Establish cuttings on bark or in a loose epiphyte medium and maintain warmth and humidity. Bright indirect light and light, frequent misting suit its climbing habit; let the roots dry a touch between waterings. It forms hollow, pouch-like leaves and small red flowers as it matures.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in warm mix indoors and grow on where temperatures stay high.

directions: Barely cover fresh seed in gritty, sterile mix and keep it warm and lightly moist until sprouts appear within a couple of weeks. Move seedlings to full sun and a fast-draining pot, watering deeply but only when dry and cutting back sharply in cool weather. The swollen basal caudex thickens with age and trumpet-shaped pink to red flowers open through the warm season.

planting.note: Start seed in hot, well-drained mix and grow seedlings in strong sun.

directions: Sow seed thinly on top of a gritty medium and keep it warm until germination in one to two weeks. This species builds an especially fat, squat base, so pot in a lean mix and water only when thoroughly dry. Give it maximum sun for the sturdiest caudex and clusters of pink blooms in the warm months.

planting.note: Germinate seed warm and grow the slender caudex plants in full sun.

directions: Press seed into warm, sharply drained mix and keep barely damp until seedlings emerge. This species tends to bloom young and long, so give it full sun and stingy watering once established. Soft pink flowers appear repeatedly through the growing season above a modestly swollen base.

planting.note: Sow seed warm and overwinter dry, as this species drops leaves and blooms in the cool dry period.

directions: Start fresh seed in a hot, free-draining medium and grow seedlings on in full sun. Water generously in active growth but keep the plant nearly dry while it is leafless and dormant. Striking white-edged crimson flowers open on bare stems, often in the cooler dry season.

planting.note: Germinate seed in warm, gritty mix and grow slowly in strong light.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in a mineral-rich, sharp-draining mix kept warm and lightly moist for germination. Grow the plant hard in full sun with careful watering, keeping it dry during winter dormancy. It swells into a bottle-shaped silvery caudex and lifts bright yellow flowers on tall stalks in spring.

planting.note: Start seed warm and rest the plant dry through winter.

directions: Germinate seed in a warm, gritty medium and grow the spiny plant in full sun with excellent drainage. Water in the warm season and withhold it while the plant is leafless in winter. A squat, spiny caudex supports white star-shaped flowers, sometimes pink-tinged, in late summer to autumn.

planting.note: Sow warm and keep the large root caudex mostly buried while young.

directions: Start seed in gritty mix at warm temperatures and grow the thin, spiny stems in full sun. Water moderately in growth and keep dry in dormancy, raising the swollen root caudex over the years for display. Pink-striped tubular flowers cover the branch tips in the warm season.

planting.note: Germinate seed warm and grow the swollen-rooted plant in bright light.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in mineral mix and keep warm until sprouting. The plant forms a fat, turnip-like base with slender twiggy stems bearing paired spines, so pot it lean and water only in active growth. Pale pink to greenish bell flowers open in the warm months, with a dry winter rest.

planting.note: Start seed in warm gritty mix and grow slowly in full sun.

directions: Germinate seed warm in a sharp-draining medium and grow the plant hard in strong sun. It builds a rounded silvery caudex with short spiny branches; water in growth and keep dry when dormant. Cup-shaped yellow flowers on long stalks distinguish it from its close relatives.

planting.note: Sow seed warm; this tree-like species takes many years to bloom.

directions: Start seed in a warm, gritty mix and grow the columnar, spiny trunk in the brightest light available. Water regularly in summer and keep it dry and warm through winter to avoid rot. It matures into a slim silvery-barked tree topped with narrow leaves, eventually bearing white flowers.

planting.note: Germinate seed warm and grow the flattened caudex plant in full sun.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in mineral mix kept warm until germination. The plant forms a low, spiny, grey-green caudex; water in the warm season and rest it dry in winter. Clusters of bright yellow flowers rise on short stalks above the crown in spring.

planting.note: This winter-grower is sown in autumn and rested in the hot summer months.

directions: Sow seed in a very open mineral mix and germinate cool, as this species grows in the winter rainfall period. Give it maximum light and careful watering during the cool season, keeping it bone dry in summer. It slowly forms a tall, spine-covered stem crowned with a tuft of leaves and dull red-brown flowers.

planting.note: Sow warm; this flat, slow species needs patience and perfect drainage.

directions: Start seed in a lean, extremely gritty medium and keep warm and only lightly moist. This species hugs the ground as a lumpy, pancake-like caudex, so it demands full sun and very sparing water to avoid rot. Bright yellow flowers appear low over the body in spring on established plants.

planting.note: Germinate seed hot and grow the bottle-based plant in strong warmth and light.

directions: Sow seed in gritty mix at high temperatures and grow the plant in full sun with generous summer warmth. It swells to a bulbous base with spiny branches; water in growth and keep dry and warm in winter. Vivid red flowers with paler centers appear on mature specimens, unusual among the genus.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in bright, warm conditions.

directions: Lay cuttings on fast-draining succulent mix and keep the leaf nodes in contact with the surface until rooted. Provide bright light, ideally some direct morning sun, and water only when fully dry. The curved, dolphin-shaped leaves stay plumpest under good light, and small brush-like white flowers may form in cooler months.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm, bright conditions for fast establishment.

directions: Trail cuttings across gritty mix and press the stems down so they root quickly. Strong sun stresses the purple stems and bean-shaped leaves into their deepest ruby-red color, while too little light keeps them green. Water sparingly, and enjoy the small daisy-like yellow flowers that open in bright conditions.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, dry conditions after letting the cut ends callus.

directions: Let cuttings of the pale, pencil-thick stems callus for a day or two, then set them in gritty mix to root. Grow in full sun with lean soil and infrequent watering; the plant sheds its small leaves during dry spells and gets by on its green stems. It forms an upright, branching shrub with pale-cream flowers in the warm season.

planting.note: Root callused stem cuttings in gritty mix during warm weather.

directions: Callus cuttings of the thick jointed stems, then plant them in a fast-draining mineral mix. Grow in full sun with careful watering; the narrow leaves drop during the summer rest period and regrow in the cool season. It becomes a candelabra-like shrub topped with clusters of small cream flower heads.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings of the upright blue-green stems in gritty mix.

directions: Allow cuttings to callus, then root them in sharp-draining succulent mix in a bright spot. Full sun and lean watering keep the chalky blue, finger-like leaves upright and compact. This robust selection forms a bushy mound and bears small cream flower heads in the warm season.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings only once nights stay reliably above 50F.

directions: Root stem tip cuttings in warm, barely moist mix and pot on into well-drained soil. To trigger the colorful bracts, give the plant fourteen hours of complete darkness each night for about eight weeks starting in fall. Pinch young shoots through summer for a bushier plant, and expect peak color from late fall into winter.

planting.note: Transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into light, free-draining soil or containers once frost has passed. The airy cloud of tiny white bracts needs no deadheading and blooms nonstop through the warm months. Allow the surface to dry slightly between waterings, and shear lightly if plants sprawl.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil has warmed to at least 60F.

directions: Transplant rooted cuttings into well-drained beds or mixed containers once nights are warm. This fuller-flowered selection carries a dense snowfall of small white bracts from late spring until frost with no deadheading. Water when the top inch dries and give it plenty of sun for heaviest bloom.

planting.note: Move seedlings outdoors after the last frost when soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and set transplants out after frost into free-draining soil. Plants form a low mound smothered in tiny white star-like bracts all season without deadheading. Let the soil dry slightly between waterings and site in full sun for the best show.

planting.note: Sow or transplant in spring into sharply drained, gritty soil.

directions: Plant in lean, fast-draining soil where it can bake in full sun. Stiff blue-gray stems spiral upward and are topped in late winter to spring by chartreuse-yellow flower clusters. Cut spent bloom stems to the base after flowering, and once established water very sparingly.

planting.note: Pot up grafted plants in spring and keep above 50F year-round.

directions: Grow this fan-shaped crested form, usually grafted onto a rootstock, in gritty cactus mix with excellent drainage. Give bright indirect light indoors and water only when the soil is fully dry. It rarely flowers indoors and is prized instead for its coral-like ruffled ridge.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into well-drained soil in a frost-free, sunny position. Like poinsettia it is short-day sensitive, veiling itself in fragrant white bracts and foliage during late fall and early winter. Prune after flowering to shape, and protect entirely from frost.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring and keep warm and bright.

directions: Grow this thornless relative of crown-of-thorns in gritty, fast-draining mix and bright light. Root cuttings after letting the cut end callus for a few days. Given warmth and sun it produces glossy leaves and cheerful red flower clusters on and off much of the year; water only when the mix dries.

planting.note: Pot up in spring in gritty succulent mix; keep frost-free.

directions: Prize this small clumping species for its blue-green stems edged with rusty-red spines that resemble a tiny cactus. Grow in very well-drained mineral mix with strong light and water sparingly in the warm season. Small golden-yellow flowers appear along the ridges in spring.

planting.note: Pot rooted cuttings in spring; keep warm all year.

directions: This oddity has flat, mottled brown-gray stems that genuinely look withered, hence its name. Grow in gritty, fast-draining mix with bright light and let it dry thoroughly between waterings. Tiny reddish flowers dot the stem margins in the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained soil; keep well above freezing.

directions: This candelabra-form tree develops a stout trunk and upswept four-angled branches over many years. Grow in gritty, free-draining soil in strong sun and water only during active growth. Handle carefully, as the milky sap is a strong irritant; small yellow flowers line the ridges in season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty soil; protect from any frost.

directions: Clustering upright four- to five-angled stems form dense candelabra colonies over time. Grow in very sharply drained mineral soil in full sun, watering only in warm weather. Small reddish-brown flowers appear along the ridges; the caustic sap warrants careful handling.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring after callusing; keep frost-free.

directions: This fast, tree-like species forms tall green four-angled columns branching into a candelabra. Let cuttings callus for a week before rooting in gritty mix. Grow in full sun with sparing water, and keep it well away from frost; the milky latex is a strong skin and eye irritant.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in gritty mix; keep above 50F.

directions: Branching three- or four-angled green stems carry paired small spines along their wavy ridges. Grow in fast-draining soil in strong light, watering only when dry during the warm season. Handle with care because of the caustic sap; keep entirely frost-free.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings in spring; keep warm and dry in winter.

directions: A tree-forming spurge that builds a trunk topped by tiers of three-angled branches like a green chandelier. Root cuttings after callusing and grow in gritty, well-drained soil with full sun. Water sparingly, protect from frost, and take care with the irritant milky sap.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings in spring; keep frost-free.

directions: This large tree euphorbia carries three-angled branches lined with prominent tooth-like ridges. Grow in sharply drained soil in full sun and water only during active growth. Keep it above freezing and handle carefully because of the caustic latex.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in gritty mix; keep dry and frost-free in winter.

directions: This rounded shrub is built of smooth, pencil-like green stems that turn tipped with clusters of bright yellow flower heads in spring. Grow in very free-draining soil in full sun and water only sparingly. Root cuttings once the cut ends have dried and callused.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring into gritty succulent mix.

directions: A clustering dwarf that forms a low mound of knobby, many-angled green stems crowded with soft tubercles. Grow in gritty mineral mix with bright light and minimal water. Detach and root the freely produced offsets to make new plants; small yellow-green flowers appear in the warm season.

planting.note: Pot in spring in deep, gritty mix; keep dry in winter.

directions: This species grows from a swollen underground caudex that sends up flattened, toothed green stems banded in reddish tones. Plant in a deep pot of very free-draining mix and expose part of the caudex once established. Water lightly in the warm months and keep bone dry and frost-free in winter.

planting.note: Root offsets in spring in gritty mix; keep frost-free.

directions: Named for its clustering globular green stem segments, this small spurge slowly forms a mounded colony. Grow in sharply drained succulent mix with bright light and sparse water. It sends up slender stalks topped with small yellow-green flowers during the growing season.

planting.note: Divide in spring into gritty mix; keep dry in cool weather.

directions: This medusoid species radiates many slender green arms from a central caudex, forming a low crown. Grow in gritty, free-draining mix in bright light with cautious watering. Keep it dry and warm through winter; small honey-scented flowers form near the center in season.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings in spring; keep frost-free and dry in winter.

directions: This variegated form has ribbed, knobby stems in soft creamy white flushed with pink in cool bright light. Grow in gritty succulent mix and water only when fully dry. Let cuttings callus before rooting, and give it strong light to keep the coloring vivid.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring or fall in shade or part shade.

directions: One of the few spurges that thrives in dry shade, spreading slowly by underground runners into a glossy dark-green groundcover. Plant divisions in spring or fall and let it colonize under trees. Lime-green flower heads rise in spring; cut spent stems to the base and wear gloves against the sap.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: This selection forms bold evergreen domes of blue-gray leaves topped in spring by large, rounded heads of golden-yellow-green bracts. Plant in free-draining soil in full sun and shelter from harsh winter wind. Cut flowered stems right to the base once they fade, wearing gloves against the milky sap.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharp-draining soil and full sun.

directions: A compact selection valued for its intensely blue-gray foliage and large chartreuse spring flower heads. Grow in lean, free-draining soil in full sun for the best leaf color. Remove each flowered stem at ground level after bloom, and handle carefully because of the irritant sap.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil, full sun to light shade.

directions: A dwarf, mounding hybrid with dark stems, red-tinged evergreen leaves, and lime bracts holding dark red central eyes. Plant in free-draining soil in sun or light shade for a tidy front-of-border cushion. Shear off spent flower stems after bloom, wearing gloves against the sap.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: This selection emerges with deep purple spring foliage that contrasts against bright chartreuse-yellow flower heads. Plant in well-drained average soil in full sun where it forms a neat rounded dome. Trim the plant back lightly after flowering to keep it compact, and wear gloves when cutting.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: A quietly attractive woodland-edge spurge forming low clumps of green foliage that colors well in autumn. Plant in moist but well-drained soil in dappled shade or morning sun. Small greenish-yellow flowers appear in late spring, and it self-sows gently where happy.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in well-drained soil in sun or part shade.

directions: This softly hairy perennial carries reddish stems and green leaves topped through summer by long-lasting sprays of yellow bracts. Grow in free-draining soil in sun or light shade. It self-seeds readily, so remove faded heads if you want to limit spread; wear gloves when handling.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: This Mediterranean shrub forms a rounded dome that flushes with domes of yellow-green bracts in spring, then drops its leaves to rest through summer drought. Grow in lean, very free-draining soil in full sun. Water little once established, and expect the foliage to color red before its summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in a sheltered sunny spot.

directions: An architectural shrub with bare lower stems topped by whorls of narrow blue-green leaves and rounded lime flower heads in spring. Plant in free-draining soil in a warm, sheltered position with full sun. Root cuttings after the cut ends callus, and protect from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty soil in full sun; protect from frost.

directions: This shrubby Canary Island spurge forms mounds of green semi-succulent stems tipped with yellow bracts in late winter and spring. Grow in very free-draining soil in full sun and water sparingly. Let cuttings callus before rooting, and shelter it from frost and cold wet.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings in spring; keep dry and frost-free in winter.

directions: This species slowly builds a low, dome-shaped colony of short spiny ribbed stems. Grow in sharply drained mineral mix in full sun and water only in warm weather. Keep it completely dry and above freezing in winter, and take care with the caustic latex.

planting.note: Plant in spring in very sharp-draining gritty soil in full sun.

directions: This tight, spiny Mediterranean cushion turns golden with bracts in spring, then bleaches to a silvery dome of dead-looking twigs in summer drought. Grow in gritty, very free-draining soil in full sun. Water sparingly and give it a hot, dry site to mimic its rocky homeland.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings in spring; keep frost-free and dry in winter.

directions: This South African succulent forms clusters of finger-like gray-green stems studded with the persistent bases of old leaves. Grow in gritty, free-draining mix in full sun with minimal water. It produces curious grayish-pink flowers on short stalks; keep it dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Pot in spring in gritty mix; keep dry and frost-free in winter.

directions: This ball-shaped succulent has a patterned green body ridged with low ribs and retains dried flower stalks that give it a persistent crown. Grow in very free-draining mineral mix in bright light and water sparingly during the warm months. Keep it bone dry through winter and safe from frost.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in gritty mix; keep frost-free.

directions: This succulent shrublet grows a thick stem crowned with a rosette of soft green leaves that drop as it flowers. Grow in free-draining mix in full sun to light shade and water moderately in the warm season. It sends up honey-scented yellow-green flower heads on tall stalks in spring.

planting.note: Pot grafted or rooted crests in spring; keep frost-free.

directions: The crested form of the medusa-head spurge fuses its arms into wavy green fans and brain-like ridges. Grow in gritty succulent mix in bright indirect light and water only when dry. Often grown grafted; keep it warm and dry in winter and protect from any frost.

planting.note: Pot in spring in deep gritty mix; keep dry and frost-free in winter.

directions: This medusoid species radiates many finger-like green arms from a stout caudex, forming a low starburst. Grow in a deep pot of very free-draining mineral mix in strong light. Water lightly only in active growth and keep it bone dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Pot in spring in deep gritty mix; keep dry in cool weather.

directions: This species grows a club-shaped succulent stem from an underground rootstock, topped with a tuft of leaves in the growing season. Plant in a deep, free-draining pot and keep in bright light. Water moderately while in leaf, then keep dry and frost-free while dormant.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: A tough Mediterranean spurge with blue-green leaves that have distinctly saw-toothed margins and heads of yellow-green bracts. Grow in free-draining soil in full sun where it tolerates heat and drought. It can self-sow freely, so trim faded heads to keep it in check; wear gloves against the sap.

planting.note: Pot seedlings in spring in gritty mix; keep dry and frost-free in winter.

directions: A small, neatly globular succulent very similar to the baseball plant, with a ribbed green body and no spines. Grow from seed or offsets in sharply drained mineral mix with bright light. Water sparingly in the warm season only, and keep it completely dry through a frost-free winter rest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom the following year.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 to 5 inches deep in fall in fertile, free-draining soil. Broad flat domes of greenish-white starry florets, each with a dark green ovary at the center, open in early summer. Leave foliage to yellow naturally so the bulb can recharge for next season.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Bury bulbs 4 to 6 inches deep in autumn in a sheltered spot with sharp drainage. Loose umbels of pendent cream, green, and maroon bells dangle in late spring, then flip upward into striking seed heads. Allow it to self-sow, or deadhead if you want to keep it in check.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive but drained soil. Dense round heads of soft chartreuse-yellow florets rise above twisting blue-green leaves in early summer, an unusual color among ornamental onions. Bees favor the blooms, which dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Set divisions in spring in a cool, partly shaded bed.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist through summer. Broad ribbed leaves make a lush clump, and greenish-white globe umbels appear in early summer. This alpine species prefers dappled shade and dislikes hot, dry sites.

planting.note: Plant in spring for late-summer to autumn bloom.

directions: Set clumps in fertile, moisture-retaining soil in sun or light shade. Flat-topped umbels of deep wine-red to purple starry florets open unusually late, from midsummer into autumn. Keep it watered during dry spells, as it resents baking out completely.

planting.note: Plant in spring for summer bloom the same year.

directions: Plant divisions or set seedlings in ordinary, well-drained garden soil. Loose umbels of sweetly scented white stars, each with a reddish midrib, appear through summer above edible flat leaves. Deadhead to limit self-seeding, or leave heads for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant bulbils or cloves in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Plant cloves or bulbils about 2 to 3 inches deep in fall in light, well-drained soil. Compact heads mix small purple florets with dark bulbils on a curled stalk in early summer. The mildly garlicky bulbs are edible, and it naturalizes readily in a wild border.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a late-autumn display.

directions: Set plants in well-drained soil at the front of a border or in a rock garden. Neat grassy clumps produce rounded rose-purple umbels remarkably late, often in October when little else blooms. Leave the tidy foliage undisturbed and simply divide overgrown clumps in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a gritty rock-garden bed.

directions: Plant in very sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun, ideal for a trough or scree. Nodding clusters of comparatively large rose-pink bells hang in summer before turning upward. Keep it dry in winter, as this dwarf alpine dislikes soggy ground.

planting.note: Plant in spring for late-summer bloom.

directions: Plant in gritty, humus-rich soil with reliable drainage in sun or light shade. Pendent umbels of true sky-blue bells hang from short stems in late summer, a rare color for the genus. Give it a cool root run and steady moisture through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in spring for midsummer flowering.

directions: Set plants in cool, well-drained gritty soil in a rock garden or raised bed. Compact heads of nodding deep blue bells appear in summer on short slender stems. This tidy Himalayan species stays small and rewards a spot at the front of a bed.

planting.note: Plant in spring for early to midsummer bloom.

directions: Plant in ordinary well-drained soil in full sun near the edge of a border. Loose umbels of small bell-shaped purple flowers rise above grassy clumps in early summer. It is easy and free-flowering, forming neat tufts that divide readily in spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in gritty, fast-draining soil that dries out in summer. Umbels of bright rose-purple flowers with pointed, recurving tepals open in late spring. This western native needs a dry summer rest, so avoid irrigating it once it goes dormant.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for spring bloom in mild regions.

directions: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in sharply drained soil in a warm, sunny spot. Airy umbels of translucent white to pale pink cups appear in spring on slender stems. A California native, it thrives in dry-summer climates and appreciates protection where winters are cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist ground.

directions: Plant in rich soil at a pond margin or in any spot that stays reliably wet. Dense rounded umbels of rose-pink flowers top tall stems in summer. Unlike most drought-loving alliums, this western native demands steady moisture and will not tolerate drying out.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs 5 to 6 inches deep in autumn in fertile, free-draining soil. Large dense globes of deep violet-purple stars rise on sturdy stems in late spring, one of the parents of many hybrid alliums. Give the strappy leaves room, and let them die back before tidying.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in autumn in well-drained, fertile ground. Open globes of star-shaped purple-violet florets with protruding stamens appear in late spring, giving a slightly spidery look. The dried seed heads persist for weeks and add structure to the border.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Bury bulbs 4 to 5 inches deep in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil. Rounded lilac-purple umbels top tall bare stems in late spring, weaving well among perennials. Plant it in generous drifts and let the seed heads stand for a lasting sculptural effect.

planting.note: Plant in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Plant divisions in ordinary soil that stays moist but not waterlogged. Rounded umbels of pale lilac-pink flowers rise above ridged, keeled leaves in summer. This tidy clump-former tolerates damp meadow conditions and is popular with pollinators.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in autumn in well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Loose umbels of pure white starry bells open in late spring and combine nicely with other spring bulbs. It naturalizes gently and is one of the easier white-flowered species.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom in mild areas.

directions: Plant bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in sharply drained soil in a warm, sunny position. Airy umbels of white starry flowers rise above soft, hair-fringed leaves in late spring. This Mediterranean species enjoys a dry summer rest and can naturalize in mild gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a gritty rock garden.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, gritty soil in full sun in a trough or rock garden. Nodding clusters of comparatively large rose-pink bells hang gracefully in early summer and, unlike its cousin narcissiflorum, do not turn upright in fruit. Keep it on the dry side, especially over winter.

scientific_name: Allium senescens subsp. montanum var. glaucum

planting.note: Plant in spring for late-summer bloom.

directions: Plant divisions in lean, well-drained soil in full sun at the front of a bed. The gray-green leaves twist and curl into distinctive spirals, and soft pink umbels appear in late summer. It is drought-tolerant, tidy, and easily divided in spring to make more clumps.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs 5 to 6 inches deep in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil. Big, dense violet-purple globes on tall stems open in late spring above broad blue-green leaves. This large-flowered selection makes a bold statement and dries well for lasting seed heads.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in autumn in rich, free-draining soil. Very tall stems carry large rounded heads of deep lilac-purple florets in late spring to early summer. Its height makes it a fine mid-border accent that rises above surrounding perennials.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 5 inches deep in autumn in well-drained, fertile ground. Dense purple-violet globes appear in late spring on sturdy upright stems. It holds its color well and, like other tall alliums, leaves attractive skeletal seed heads as it fades.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Bury bulbs 5 inches deep in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil. Rounded heads of star-shaped deep violet florets with a slightly spidery look open in early summer. Plant in groups for the strongest effect and allow the seed heads to persist into late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 to 5 inches deep in autumn in well-drained soil. Compact rounded umbels of rich purple stars appear in late spring on medium stems. Its moderate height suits the middle of a border, and the dried heads add lasting interest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs 5 to 6 inches deep in autumn in fertile, sharply drained soil. Dense globes of deep violet-red florets open in early summer, blooming a touch later than many big alliums. Deep color and strong stems make it a reliable cut flower and dried seed head.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in autumn in well-drained soil. Neat, dense white globes on short stems open in late spring, holding tidy foliage that stays low and green. Its compact size makes it ideal for the front of a border or a rock garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early-summer bloom.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil. Loose umbels of bright carmine-pink bells appear in early summer on very short stems. This dwarf selection is perfect for rock gardens and troughs and naturalizes gently over time.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed receives natural cold stratification over winter.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it into gritty, fast-draining soil, giving it a cold-moist period to break dormancy. Site it in full sun with sharp drainage, as the clasping blue-green foliage rots in wet ground. Watch for tall spikes of coral-scarlet tubular flowers that draw hummingbirds in late spring.

planting.note: Fall sowing gives the moist chilling this Texas native prefers before spring bloom.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of lean limestone-based soil and barely cover, keeping it cool and moist until sprouts appear. Plant into full sun where drainage is perfect, since it thrives on rocky slopes and resents rich beds. Rosy-pink to red blooms with darker nectar guides open across mid to late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors after a cold-moist chill, then set out once soil warms.

directions: Press the fine seed onto gritty mix and provide a short cold period to improve germination. This shrubby, leathery-leaved species demands blazing sun and rocky, alkaline drainage, tolerating extreme heat once established. It flushes glowing red tubular flowers from summer well into fall, especially after monsoon-style watering.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for winter stratification and a strong spring stand.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist stratification, then surface-sow and keep barely covered until germination. Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun where the waxy blue-gray rosettes stay dry at the crown. Deep cardinal-red bells rise on sturdy stems in early summer and are irresistible to hummingbirds.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the last frost into hot, dry ground.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in sharp, sandy mix, giving a brief chill to boost sprouting. This tall desert species needs relentless sun and gravelly drainage, sulking in heavy or moist soils. Towering wands of large scarlet trumpets appear over silvery foliage in spring, feeding hummingbirds for weeks.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out after frost into lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Barely cover the small seed and keep it cool and evenly moist until it germinates. Set plants in gritty soil and full sun, providing excellent drainage to prevent crown rot. Loose spikes of rose-pink tubular flowers open in spring above tidy blue-green rosettes.

planting.note: Autumn sowing supplies the cold-moist chill this Great Basin native needs.

directions: Stratify seed with a cold-moist period, then surface-sow onto gritty, mineral soil. Grow in full sun with fast drainage, keeping the blue-gray foliage on the dry side. Vivid rose-red to magenta trumpets appear early in spring, a striking desert bloomer for xeric gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling, then set out into deep sandy soil once warm.

directions: Germination can be slow and erratic, so give seed a cold-moist chill and be patient. This bushy, phlox-like species craves deep sand and full sun and dislikes root disturbance once settled. It forms a rounded shrub covered in pale pink and white flowers through summer, thriving in the harshest heat.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed gets prolonged cold before germinating in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty rock-garden mix, keeping it cool. This ground-hugging alpine spreads into a tight green mat and needs full sun with impeccable drainage. Short-stemmed lavender-blue flowers stud the mat in late spring, ideal for troughs and crevice gardens.

planting.note: Fall sowing provides the winter chill needed for even germination.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist stratification before surface-sowing into gritty soil. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage, mimicking the gravelly montane slopes it calls home. Deep blue to violet-blue flowers with a white throat open in early summer above compact foliage.

planting.note: Sow in autumn to satisfy the seed's cold-moist requirement.

directions: Stratify and surface-sow the seed, keeping the mix cool and lightly moist. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil where it withstands cold and drought. Showy sky-blue to violet trumpets crowd the upper stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Autumn sowing supplies the extended chill this prairie species prefers.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty, alkaline mix. This early bloomer wants full sun and dry, mineral soil, resenting summer irrigation. Intense sky-blue flowers open very early over waxy, blue-gray leaves, one of the first penstemons to flower.

planting.note: Fall sow for winter chilling and reliable spring emergence.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist, then surface-sow and keep barely covered until sprouts show. Grow in full sun to light shade in well-drained soil that stays a touch cooler than typical desert penstemons. Tidy whorls of small creamy-yellow flowers rise in late spring, unusual for the genus.

planting.note: Sow in autumn into deep sandy soil for natural cold stratification.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist chill and surface-sow into fast-draining sandy mix. This species insists on deep sand, blazing sun, and dry conditions once established. Dense spikes of blue to lavender flowers rise over thick blue-gray leaves in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Autumn sowing gives the prolonged cold this western native requires.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty, well-drained mix. Grow in full sun on lean, dry soil, avoiding summer water that shortens its life. Large, brilliant blue trumpets crowd the stems in late spring, one of the showiest blue penstemons.

planting.note: Fall sow so the seed chills over winter for spring germination.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into gritty soil. This adaptable species tolerates rocky walls and lean ground in full sun with good drainage. Airy sprays of rosy-lavender flowers with toothed leaves appear from midsummer into fall, a long bloomer for dry gardens.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for winter chilling and vigorous spring seedlings.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty, well-drained mix. This robust, easygoing species handles slopes and lean soils in full sun to light shade. Dense showy spikes of lavender to purple-blue flowers open in early to midsummer over toothed foliage.

planting.note: Autumn sowing supplies the cold-moist period seed needs.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into sharply drained, rocky mix. It grows on hot, dry scablands, so give it full sun and gravelly soil with no summer irrigation. Small cream to pale yellow flowers with fine purple lines cluster on the stems in late spring.

planting.note: Fall sow so seed chills fully before germinating in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty rock-garden mix; germination may be slow. This shrubby, mat-forming alpine wants full sun and lean, perfectly drained soil among rocks. Rose-pink to cherry-red flowers cover the mounded evergreen foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for the cold stratification this California native prefers.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into gritty, fast-draining mix. Plant in full sun on lean, dry ground, avoiding rich beds and summer water. Deep azure-blue trumpets over blue-gray foliage light up the garden from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Autumn sowing gives the winter chill needed for germination.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty, mineral soil. This short prairie species needs full sun and dry, sharply drained ground and dislikes irrigation. Large lavender to purple flowers with a conspicuously hairy staminode open in late spring above low, hairy foliage.

planting.note: Fall sow so seed receives natural winter stratification.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into lean, well-drained mix. A tough Great Plains native, it thrives in full sun and dry, alkaline soils with no coddling. Short spikes of white flowers, often lightly lined with lavender, open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for prolonged cold and even spring germination.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow into gritty, sandy soil. This early-blooming plains native demands full sun and fast drainage and resents wet soil. Dense clusters of sky-blue to lavender flowers rise over narrow blue-gray leaves in spring.

planting.note: Fall sowing supplies the cold-moist chill for good germination.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist, then surface-sow into gritty, well-drained soil. Grow in full sun on lean, dry ground where the blue-gray rosettes stay dry. Spikes of pink to lavender-blue flowers, held mostly to one side, open in late spring.

planting.note: Autumn sow for winter chilling and strong spring seedlings.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty, well-drained mix. This graceful species grows easily in full sun and lean soil, tolerating some clay if drainage is good. Slender wands of pale blue to lavender flowers, often lined inside, bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in fall so the seed chills fully over winter.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into gritty, well-drained soil. This vigorous Rocky Mountain species grows in full sun on lean ground and takes cold in stride. Dense spikes of intense blue flowers open in early summer, one of the richest blues among penstemons.

planting.note: Autumn sowing gives the cold-moist chill this desert species needs.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow into sharply drained, gritty mix. Grow in full sun on dry, alkaline ground where the thick blue-gray leaves stay dry. Spikes of blue to lavender flowers rise in late spring, a durable choice for xeric plantings.

planting.note: Fall sow to give seed a natural chilling period.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow onto gritty mix. This low, sub-shrubby species forms silvery-green mounds and needs full sun with excellent drainage. Lavender-blue flowers stud the fine foliage in early summer, a tidy front-of-border or rock-garden plant.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed chills before spring germination.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto gritty rock-garden mix. This creeping, needle-leaved species forms low evergreen mats and demands full sun with sharp drainage. Sky-blue to violet flowers cover the mat in early summer, excellent among rocks and in troughs.

planting.note: Fall sowing supplies the winter chill for germination.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into gritty alpine mix. This mat-forming montane species needs full sun and impeccable drainage among rocks. Lavender-blue flowers rise just above the gray-green cushion in early summer.

planting.note: Autumn sow so seed receives the cold-moist period it needs.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow onto well-drained, gritty mix. This easy, adaptable species grows in full sun on lean soil and self-sows gently where happy. Slender spikes of blue to blue-purple flowers open in early summer over neat green foliage.

planting.note: Fall sow for winter chilling and reliable emergence.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into well-drained soil. Unlike most penstemons, this meadow native tolerates slightly moister ground and grows in full sun to light shade. Compact whorls of blue-purple flowers rise in early to midsummer on upright stems.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for the cold-moist chill this plains native prefers.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow into gritty, sandy soil. Grow in full sun on lean, dry ground where the blue-gray clasping leaves stay dry. Whorls of blue-purple flowers open in late spring over the distinctive glaucous foliage.

planting.note: Autumn sowing gives seed its needed cold stratification.

directions: Stratify seed cold-moist and surface-sow into sharply drained, gritty mix. This southwestern native wants full sun and dry, lean soil with no summer water. Lilac to lavender flowers with a bearded staminode open in late spring above low foliage.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out after frost into hot, dry ground.

directions: Surface-sow seed after a brief chill and keep the mix on the warm side. This tall desert species craves full sun and gravelly drainage, thriving in extreme heat. Airy tiers of coral to salmon-red flowers rise over blue-gray rosettes in spring, feeding hummingbirds.

planting.note: Fall sow so seed chills over winter before germinating.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and surface-sow into gritty, volcanic-type or sandy soil. This rare cinder-cone native grows in full sun with fast drainage and self-sows where content. Loose spikes of rose-pink flowers rise over blue-gray foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for possible first-year bloom, then transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed at warm temperatures; unlike many penstemons it germinates readily without long chilling. This Mexican species grows in full sun with good drainage and blooms over a long season. Bright cherry-red tubular flowers cover the plant from summer into fall, drawing hummingbirds.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant out once frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow seed at warm temperatures, as it germinates without a long cold period. This tender Mexican and Central American species grows in full sun with fertile, well-drained soil. Slender spikes of rosy-purple to violet bell-shaped flowers appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed warm, since this Mexican species does not require prolonged chilling. Give it full sun and well-drained soil, with shelter in colder zones where it is only marginally hardy. Long red tubular flowers with pale throats open on tall, semi-woody stems through summer and fall.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after the last frost into rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Propagate this border hybrid from softwood cuttings, since named forms do not come true from seed. Plant in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, and leave old growth over winter for crown protection in cold areas. Pure white tubular flowers, aging faintly pink, bloom from early summer into fall if deadheaded.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost into well-drained, fertile ground.

directions: Increase this border hybrid by softwood cuttings for true-to-type plants. Grow in full sun with good drainage, mulching the crown in cold zones and cutting back only in spring. Deep wine-red tubular flowers with lighter throats bloom generously from summer into autumn with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after the last frost into rich, free-draining soil.

directions: Take softwood cuttings to propagate this classic border penstemon, as it will not come true from seed. Plant in full sun with fertile, well-drained soil and protect the crown over winter in colder gardens. Large scarlet-red flowers with a white throat bloom from summer into fall when regularly deadheaded.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it into gritty, well-drained soil without covering, since light aids sprouting. Grow this grey-felted Caucasian species in full sun with sharp drainage to prevent crown rot. Nodding, soft grey-blue bells open along arching stems in early summer; shear spent stalks to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Sow in spring for a rosette that flowers the following year.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of lean, gravelly soil and leave uncovered, as it needs light to germinate. This biennial to short-lived perennial forms a rosette the first year and sends up branched stems of violet, downy-edged bells the next. Let it self-seed on a sunny bank or rock garden to keep a colony going.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; roots and leaves were once eaten, flowers follow the next year.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed in ordinary garden soil and keep evenly moist until seedlings appear. Historically grown as a root and salad vegetable, this biennial makes a leafy rosette the first season and tall, slender spikes of pale lilac bells the second. Allow a few plants to seed if you want a self-renewing patch.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer into open ground where plants are to flower.

directions: Press the dust-fine seed onto the soil surface and do not bury it, since light triggers germination. Widely branching stems carry open, star-shaped purple bells that flare outward rather than nodding, blooming through early summer. A charming meadow biennial that reseeds freely in grassy, unfussed corners.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in spring; the rosette takes a year or more before its single flowering.

directions: Sow onto gritty alpine soil and leave uncovered, giving the seed a cold spell to break dormancy. Unusual among bellflowers for its dense spike of pale straw-yellow, sweetly scented bells packed on a stout stem. It is monocarpic, dying after flowering, so let the seed ripen and scatter to replace the plant.

planting.note: Divide or plant in spring into sharply drained, gritty soil.

directions: Set young plants in humus-rich, gritty soil that never bakes or waterlogs, with a little afternoon shade in hot regions. This Austrian alpine runs underground to make a low mat, then hangs single, deep violet bells on wiry threads. Grow it in a trough or scree where its wandering habit can knit between stones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a crevice or scree with perfect drainage.

directions: Tuck plants into gritty, alkaline scree where sharp drainage keeps the crown dry through winter. This choice Italian Alps species carries surprisingly large, upfacing lilac-blue cups over a tight grey-green mat. A prize for tufa, troughs, and the alpine house rather than open borders.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny wall crevice or scree bed.

directions: Root basal cuttings in gritty compost or plant into a lean, free-draining crevice where its taproot can dive deep. Red-tinted buds open to loose sprays of nodding white to pale pink funnels over birch-like toothed leaves. This Turkish species resents winter wet, so a gravel mulch and sharp drainage are essential.

planting.note: Set out or hang after frost; often grown as a houseplant or basket plant.

directions: Root soft cuttings in a light mix and grow in bright, indirect light in containers or hanging baskets. Cascading stems smother themselves in flat, star-shaped blue or white flowers from summed into autumn. Pinch young growth for fullness and deadhead often, as this tender Italian species blooms most freely when kept picked.

planting.note: Sow in spring; expect a leafy first year and a flush of huge bells the second.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist compost and press in lightly, keeping warm until seedlings emerge. This Greek biennial is celebrated for its outsized, inflated pale blue to white bells that nearly hide the sprawling plant. Grow in full sun with good drainage and let it self-sow, since each plant flowers once and then dies.

planting.note: Sow in spring for a rosette that overwinters and flowers once the next year.

directions: Sow fine seed on the surface of gritty compost and keep barely moist in bright conditions. The first-year rosette of felted grey leaves is handsome in its own right before the tall candelabra of white to blush bells appears. Protect the crown from winter wet in a raised bed or pot, and save ripe seed since the plant is monocarpic.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in cool, well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions into humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil that stays cool at the roots. This Caucasian species slowly runs to form a colony, hanging large, deep violet-purple bells on short stems in early summer. Deadhead after the first flush and it often rewards you with a lighter second showing.

planting.note: Sow spring or autumn; plants flower from the second year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow the plants on in ordinary, well-drained soil that tolerates lime. Tall, unbranched stems carry a slender one-sided spire of small nodding lilac bells above rough grey-green foliage. A tough, drought-tolerant meadow species that suits naturalistic and gravel plantings.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring into gritty, sunny alpine soil.

directions: Surface-sow onto lean, stony soil and give the seed a cold winter to prompt germination. Closely allied to the common harebell, it dangles a few large, deep blue-violet bells on slender stems above narrow leaves. Ideal for a sunny rock garden or trough where its neat alpine habit shows to advantage.

planting.note: Sow in spring; a rosette forms the first year and a tall spike the next.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of gritty, alkaline soil and leave uncovered in a bright spot. This alpine-meadow biennial throws a dense, tapering spike closely set with small violet-blue bells. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and let seed ripen to keep the planting self-sustaining.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in cool alpine-meadow conditions.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and grow in cool, moist but well-drained soil in sun or light shade. A slender European mountain species with leafy stems and a loose one-sided cluster of nodding blue bells. Reliable and easy in a rock garden or the front of a cool border.

planting.note: Plant in spring into tufa or gritty crevices with impeccable drainage.

directions: Grow this connoisseur's alpine in gritty, limy scree or tufa where slugs cannot reach the buds. Its unmistakable pale blue bells are pinched and crimped shut at the mouth like a tiny bottle. A challenging Julian Alps species best suited to the alpine house or a well-guarded trough.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in spring; give a cold period to break dormancy.

directions: Sow onto gritty alpine mix and leave the seed exposed to winter cold before spring germination. This dwarf Carpathian species makes a rosette hung with several downy, soft blue bells on very short stems. Short-lived but freely seeding, it is best grown in a scree or trough with lean soil and full sun.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set plants into gritty, free-draining soil where the tidy clump can settle undisturbed. This refined Croatian species differs from most bellflowers in its narrow, tubular pale blue bells that dangle in nodding sprays. Neat and non-invasive, it is a fine choice for the front of a sunny border or a large trough.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed after a cold, moist stratification period.

directions: Stratify seed cold and moist, then sow onto gritty, acidic scree in a cool, bright position. This tiny arctic-alpine of Alaska and East Asia lifts large, upfacing pale blue bells over toothed rosette leaves. It demands cool summers and sharp drainage, making it a specialist plant for the alpine enthusiast.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost into a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and grow young plants on before setting out in a sunny, sharply drained bed. Branching stems carry airy, outward-facing star flowers of pale blue with a striking deep violet eye. This Balkan species is somewhat tender, so give it a warm wall and gritty soil in colder gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost into moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Sow onto the surface of moist compost and grow on until settled before planting out in spring. This robust Iberian species sends up leafy stems studded with large, open purple-blue bells over a long summer season. Give it deep, fertile soil that stays moist and a little shade from the fiercest sun.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring into gritty, well-drained rock-garden soil.

directions: Set small divisions into a limy, gritty crevice or scree where drainage is excellent. This eastern Alps species forms neat tufts and hangs slender, nodding sky-blue bells on threadlike stems. Easy and long-flowering, it is a dependable filler for troughs, walls, and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sharply drained, sunny scree.

directions: Grow this compact Croatian alpine in gritty, alkaline scree with full sun and free drainage. Unlike the nodding bells of many kin, it holds starry, upfacing lilac-blue flowers just above a tidy grey-green mat. A tidy, non-spreading plant well suited to troughs and crevice gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a gritty, well-drained rock garden.

directions: Set plants into gritty, free-draining soil where the low mat can spread over stones. This Italian alpine, a relative of the Adriatic bellflower, opens flat, star-shaped violet-blue flowers over toothed foliage. Grow in sun or light shade with sharp drainage and shear lightly after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in cool, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow this Caucasian species in cool, humus-rich soil that does not dry out in high summer. It is prized among bellflowers for its unusual soft creamy-yellow, nodding bells carried on leafy stems. Deadhead spent stalks and lift and divide the clump every few years to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant out or hang after frost; often grown in baskets and walls.

directions: Root soft cuttings in gritty compost and grow this trailing Italian species in a wall crevice, trough, or basket. Brittle, spreading stems carry masses of pale blue, white-centered star flowers through late summer. It resents winter wet and hard frost, so give sharp drainage and shelter or overwinter cuttings under glass.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in a sunny, well-drained wall or edge.

directions: Divide the mat in spring and plant into gritty, free-draining soil at a wall top or path edge. This selection carries flat, deep lavender-blue stars each marked with a distinct white central eye over a spreading cushion. Trim lightly after the long summer bloom to keep the low mound neat and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in fertile, well-drained border soil.

directions: Set plants in fertile, well-drained soil in sun or light shade and stake tall stems in exposed spots. Slender wands carry large, outward-facing pure white cups above evergreen basal rosettes in early summer. Deadhead each spent flower along the spike to prolong the display and prevent excess self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in sun or part shade and give room for its running roots to wander. Long, tubular ivory bells hang heavily flushed and freckled with deep crimson-red inside and out. Deadhead spent stems and lift and split the clump every few years to keep the colony in bounds.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in moist, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Set divisions into cool, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade where the spreading roots can colonize. This selection is distinctive for its split, star-shaped white bells that look almost double and dangle from arching stems. Deadhead regularly and divide every few years, as the plant spreads steadily by underground runners.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Grow in deep, rich soil that stays moist, giving support to the tall leafy stems in windy sites. Broad, branching heads of small starry white bells crown the plant through mid to late summer. Cut back after the first flush and the plant often produces a second, lighter flowering.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring at a wall top, bank, or path edge.

directions: Plant this vigorous trailer where its long stems can cascade over a wall or knit across a bank. Sprays of milky-white stars with the faintest blue flush smother the spreading mats through summer. Shear back hard after the main flush to freshen the foliage and prompt more bloom.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring into gritty, well-drained rock-garden soil.

directions: Set divisions into gritty, free-draining soil where the running roots can weave a low carpet. Countless dainty, nodding sky-blue thimble bells hang above a fine mat of small rounded leaves. Ideal between paving, in troughs, or along a path edge; trim after flowering to keep it compact.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in fertile, well-drained border soil.

directions: Plant into fertile, well-drained soil in sun or light shade and lift and divide clumps every few years. Stiff, self-supporting stems are set closely with large, upward-facing amethyst-pink cups along their length. Cut stems to the base after flowering to tidy the evergreen rosettes and discourage flopping.

planting.note: Start indoors early; this F1 series can flower in its first year.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm, moist compost and press in without covering, since light aids germination. Unlike ordinary Canterbury bells, this fast F1 selection flowers the first year, raising sturdy spikes of large violet-blue cups. Deadhead spent bells to extend the show and give the tall stems light support in windy sites.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and move seedlings out after the last hard frost.

directions: Sow shallowly and barely cover the seed, keeping the tray lightly moist until sprouts appear. Set plants in gritty, sharply drained soil where they can send up their tall stems topped with dense magenta clusters. Cut spent stalks after the summer flush to tidy the clump and encourage a second showing.

planting.note: Begin indoors in late winter and transplant once frost danger has passed.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a lean, free-draining mix and keep just moist until germination. Plant into hot, dry borders where the wiry stems can float their tight heads of deep blood-red florets above lower planting. Leave the airy seed heads standing for structure, or shear back to prompt fresh bloom.

planting.note: Seed benefits from a cold spell, so sow in autumn or chill before spring sowing.

directions: This tight alpine cushion needs a very gritty scree or trough with fast drainage at the crown. Nestle young plants so the mat sits flush with the surrounding grit, and top-dress with stone chips to keep the collar dry. In late spring nearly stemless pink flowers stud the green pad; avoid any winter wet.

planting.note: Sow in cool conditions and plant out into rock crevices once established.

directions: Tuck this cushion-forming pink into a sunny rock garden or wall crevice with sharp drainage. Keep the foliage mat lean and unfed so it stays dense and grey-green. Small fringed white to pale-pink flowers appear on short stems in early summer above the tufted growth.

planting.note: Start under cover and set out into gritty raised beds once frost has passed.

directions: Grow this compact Greek alpine in a raised bed or trough of sharply drained, gritty soil. The named blood-red calyx sets off the small pink-purple petals, which show a buff reverse on hot days. Keep the crown dry through winter and divide congested clumps in spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a warm, well-drained site.

directions: This glossy-leaved coastal species tolerates salt spray and lean, sandy soils better than most pinks. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and give a little more water than alpine kinds through summer. Dense heads of rose-pink flowers open in mid to late summer above the thick, shiny foliage.

planting.note: Slow-growing; set young plants out in spring into a hot, sharply drained spot.

directions: This spiny, hummock-forming pink builds a hard grey cushion that takes years to fill out. Give it baking sun, poor gritty soil, and perfect drainage, avoiding any rich feeding. Short-stemmed pink flowers dot the prickly mat in summer; protect the crown from winter wet.

planting.note: Seed needs cold stratification; sow in autumn or chill before a spring sowing.

directions: A true high-alpine that wants cool summers, gritty acidic soil, and steady moisture without waterlogging. Grow in a trough or scree bed and shade the roots with surrounding stones. Solitary bright pink flowers sit almost on the tight tuft in early summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Grow this fragrant fringed pink in full sun and light, free-draining soil. The deeply cut, almost feathery petals open pale pink to white and carry a sweet scent on warm evenings. Deadhead through summer to prolong the display and keep plants compact.

planting.note: Start under cover in late winter and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: This adaptable meadow pink grows readily in ordinary well-drained garden soil in sun. Toothed rose-pink petals, often flecked with darker spots, open singly on slender stems through summer. Shear over after flowering to keep the loose clumps neat.

planting.note: Sow indoors and move seedlings to sandy soil after frost.

directions: A dune species that thrives in poor, sandy, sharply drained soil and full sun. The grey-green mats produce fringed, softly scented pink flowers through summer. Keep it lean and dry, as rich or wet ground quickly rots the crown.

planting.note: Chill seed before spring sowing and grow on before planting into scree.

directions: This choice Carpathian alpine wants gritty, limey scree and cool root conditions. Each short stem carries a comparatively large pink flower marked with a distinct central zone of spots. Grow in a trough or raised bed and guard against winter wet and summer drought stress.

planting.note: Sow indoors and plant out into gritty soil after the last frost.

directions: Fine, needle-like grey foliage forms low mats topped by deeply fringed, fragrant white flowers. Give it full sun and very sharp drainage in a rock garden or gravel bed. The lacy blooms open in summer and carry a sweet clove-like perfume.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or divisions in spring into gritty soil.

directions: This historic clove pink is propagated from cuttings or division to keep its old maroon-and-white markings true. Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun with the neck of the plant kept dry. The intensely clove-scented double flowers open in early summer; take pipings after bloom to renew tired plants.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: An eighteenth-century garden pink grown for its soft shell-pink double flowers and grey foliage. Increase it from cuttings, as seed will not reproduce the variety. Grow in full sun and free-draining, neutral to alkaline soil, and deadhead to extend the fragrant summer display.

planting.note: Set out cuttings or divisions in spring into gritty, sunny beds.

directions: This old white pink, also known as Green Eyes, is prized for single blooms with a distinctive green central eye. Propagate only from cuttings to hold the variety true. Grow in full sun and sharp drainage, and remove faded flowers to encourage a longer scented season.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining soil in sun.

directions: A historic laced pink whose petals are patterned with contrasting markings on a pale ground. Keep it going from cuttings to preserve the antique lacing. Give full sun, lean alkaline soil, and good drainage, and take pipings in mid-summer to maintain vigorous stock.

planting.note: Sow indoors well ahead of the last frost; plants often bloom in their second year from an early start.

directions: This old strain of border carnation grows readily from seed and comes in a mix of clear reds, pinks, yellows, and whites. Start early indoors, then set plants in fertile, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun. Stake the tall stems and cut the spicy, fully double flowers for the vase as they open.

planting.note: Start indoors early; best bloom often comes the year after sowing.

directions: A Grenadin-type carnation grown for its very dark maroon, almost black, double flowers. Sow indoors early, grow on cool, and plant into rich, free-draining alkaline soil in full sun. Support the flower stems and pick the fragrant blooms for cutting as they unfurl.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring into sharply drained, sunny beds.

directions: One of the oldest clove-scented pinks in cultivation, carrying deep rose-red double flowers with a strong spicy fragrance. Propagate only from cuttings to keep the variety, as it will not come true from seed. Plant in full sun and gritty alkaline soil, and take pipings each summer to keep a healthy supply of young plants.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining soil.

directions: This single pink is loved for its pale flowers with a distinct dark, squared central eye and a light scent. Increase it from cuttings to keep the markings consistent. Grow in full sun and sharp drainage, deadheading regularly to prolong its long summer flowering.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant around the last frost for early summer bloom.

directions: This fragrant F1 China pink flowers quickly from seed and is often grown as an annual or short-lived perennial. Barely cover the seed and keep warm until it sprouts, then grow on cool and plant into fertile, well-drained soil. A wide range of single flowers in pink, red, and white shades open over a long season if deadheaded.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and plant out after frost, or divide in spring.

directions: Also sold under its German name Leuchtfunk, this maiden pink forms a spreading mat covered in vivid carmine-red flowers. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil where it can knit across the front of a border or rockery. Shear the whole mat lightly after the main flush to keep it dense and prompt more bloom.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for first-year flowering, then transplant after frost.

directions: A free-flowering maiden pink that blooms the first year from an early sowing, smothering its low green mats in rose-red flowers. Barely cover the fine seed and keep lightly moist until germination. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, shearing after bloom to tidy the carpet.

planting.note: Start indoors for early flowering and transplant after the last frost.

directions: This maiden pink carries bright cerise-red single flowers over spreading grassy foliage. Sow the tiny seed on the surface and press in gently, keeping warm until it sprouts. Plant in full sun and sharp drainage as edging or groundcover, and trim over after flowering for repeat bloom.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or divisions in spring into gritty soil.

directions: This tufted pink bears clean white flowers each stamped with a crisp cherry-red central ring. Grow from cuttings or division to keep the eye pattern true, planting into full sun and sharp drainage. The neat blue-grey mat repeat flowers through summer if regularly deadheaded.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining, sunny beds.

directions: Sold as Fire Star, this compact pink produces bold red flowers with a darker central zone above grey-green foliage. Propagate from cuttings, since it is a named clone. Grow in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil, and deadhead often to keep the long display going.

planting.note: Set out potted plants or divisions in spring into rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Part of the Fruit Punch series, this pink shows bicolor blooms in soft pink with a deeper rose eye above blue-green foliage. Grow from division or plant potted stock, since named cultivars do not come true from seed. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, deadheading to encourage repeat waves of fragrant flowers.
